name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ثُمَّ بَدَا لَهُم مِّنۢ بَعْدِ مَا رَأَوُا۟ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُۥ حَتَّىٰ حِينٍ (٣٥)
```

```english
Then it appeared to them, after they had seen all the signs [of his innocence], that they should confine him for some time. (35)
```

```arabic
وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ ٱلسِّجْنَ فَتَيَانِ ۖ قَالَ أَحَدُهُمَآ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَعْصِرُ خَمْرًا ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰنِىٓ أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِى خُبْزًا تَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ ۖ نَبِّئْنَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦٓ ۖ إِنَّا نَرَىٰكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
There entered the prison two youths along with him. One of them said, ‘I dreamt that I am pressing grapes.’ The other said, ‘I dreamt that I am carrying bread on my head from which the birds are eating.’ ‘Inform us of its interpretation,’ [they said], ‘for indeed we see you to be a virtuous man.’ (36)
```

```arabic
قَالَ لَا يَأْتِيكُمَا طَعَامٌ تُرْزَقَانِهِۦٓ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُمَا بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ قَبْلَ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمَا ۚ ذَٰلِكُمَا مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِى رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنِّى تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لَّا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَهُم بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘Before the meals you are served come to you I will inform you of its interpretation. That is among things my Lord has taught me. Indeed, I renounce the creed of the people who have no faith in Allah and who [also] disbelieve in the Hereafter. (37)
```

```arabic
وَٱتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ ءَابَآءِىٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ مَا كَانَ لَنَآ أَن نُّشْرِكَ بِٱللَّهِ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ مِن فَضْلِ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْنَا وَعَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
I follow the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob. It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah. That is by virtue of Allah’s grace upon us and upon all mankind, but most people do not give thanks. (38)
```

```arabic
يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ ءَأَرْبَابٌ مُّتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْوَٰحِدُ ٱلْقَهَّارُ (٣٩)
```

```english
O my prison mates! Are different masters better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount? (39)
```

```arabic
مَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءً سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِنِ ٱلْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ ۚ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِيَّاهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٠)
```

```english
You do not worship besides Him but [mere] names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah. He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion, but most people do not know. (40)
```

```arabic
يَـٰصَـٰحِبَىِ ٱلسِّجْنِ أَمَّآ أَحَدُكُمَا فَيَسْقِى رَبَّهُۥ خَمْرًا ۖ وَأَمَّا ٱلْـَٔاخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ ٱلطَّيْرُ مِن رَّأْسِهِۦ ۚ قُضِىَ ٱلْأَمْرُ ٱلَّذِى فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيَانِ (٤١)
```

```english
O my prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master, and as for the other, he will be crucified and vultures will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided.’ (41)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ لِلَّذِى ظَنَّ أَنَّهُۥ نَاجٍ مِّنْهُمَا ٱذْكُرْنِى عِندَ رَبِّكَ فَأَنسَىٰهُ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِۦ فَلَبِثَ فِى ٱلسِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ (٤٢)
```

```english
Then he said to the one whom he knew would be delivered from among the two: ‘Mention me to your master.’ But Satan caused him to forget mentioning [it] to his master. So he remained in the prison for several years. (42)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses encompass a portion of his story (peace be upon him), namely his entry into prison and his stay therein for several years. This served as a prelude to his complete proximity to the King and his attainment of the high status of Egypt. Within this period is his call in prison to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — which he presented with a wondrous exposition, revealing for the first time that he belonged to the family of Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما رَأَوُا الْآياتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّى حِينٍ** — *Then it occurred to them, after they had seen the signs, to imprison him for a while.* (12:35). The term "occurrence" (البداء) — *the emergence of an opinion after it was not present* — is used when one says, "It occurred to me regarding such a matter," meaning a new opinion appeared to me concerning it. The pronoun in His saying "to them" (لَهُمْ) refers to the Governor (العزيز), his wife, and those who followed them among the elite, the King's aides, and the nobility.

What is meant by "the signs" (الآيات) are the evidences and proofs indicating the innocence of Yusuf (peace be upon him) and the purity of his character from what they accused him of, such as the testimony of the infant, the tearing of the shirt from behind, and their racing to the door together. Perhaps among these signs was also the women cutting their hands upon seeing him, his steadfastness (استعصامه) — *his seeking divine protection and remaining chaste* — against their solicitation of him, and the confession of the Governor's wife to them that she had indeed solicited him but he remained chaste.

His saying "to imprison him" (لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ) contains the letter *lam* for an oath, meaning they swore and resolved that they would certainly imprison him. This is an explanation of the opinion that occurred to them. Connected to it is His saying "for a while" (حَتَّى حِينٍ). This phrase carries a sense of waiting, considering that "while" (حين) is cut off from its genitive construction (الإضافة). The meaning based on this is: they would imprison him until the widespread talk of the solicitation in the city ceased and people forgot it.

The meaning of the verse is: Then a new opinion appeared to the Governor and those following him—his wife and the rest of his advisors—regarding Yusuf, after they had seen these signs indicating his innocence and impeccability (العصمة). This opinion was to imprison him for a period of time until the talk of the solicitation, which brought them shame and disgrace (الشين), was forgotten; and they swore an oath upon that.

It appears from this that they only resolved upon this for the interest of the Governor’s household and to protect his family from the humiliation of accusation and scandal. Perhaps their purpose was also to maintain general public order in the city and not leave the people, especially the women, to be infatuated by him. For this beauty, which first captivated the Governor’s wife and the noble ladies of the city and did to them what it did, was of a nature that would inevitably cause a trial (بلوى) in the city.

However, what appears from his saying in prison to the King’s messenger: **ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَ** — *Return to your master and ask him, ‘What was the case of the women who cut their hands?’* (12:50) until the end of what he said; then the King’s saying to them: **ما خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ راوَدْتُنَّ يُوسُفَ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ** — *He said [to the women], ‘What was your business, when you sought to seduce Joseph?’* (12:51); and their saying: **حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما عَلِمْنا عَلَيْهِ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — *They said, ‘Allah forbid! We do not know of any evil in him.’* (12:51); then the saying of the Governor’s wife: **الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — *The governor’s wife said, ‘Now the truth has come to light! It was I who sought to seduce him, and he is indeed truthful.’* (12:51)—all of this indicates that the woman later misrepresented the matter to her husband and made him doubt Yusuf’s (peace be upon him) innocence. Thus, he believed the opposite of what the signs indicated or doubted it, and this was only due to her complete authority over him and her total possession of his heart and judgment.

Accordingly, his imprisonment was through her mediation or by her command to deflect people's accusations from herself and to discipline Yusuf, hoping he might submit to her and return to obeying her in what she commanded him, as she had threatened him in the presence of the women by saying: **وَلَئِنْ لَمْ يَفْعَلْ ما آمُرُهُ لَيُسْجَنَنَّ وَلَيَكُوناً مِنَ الصَّاغِرِينَ** — *and if he does not do what I bid him, he shall surely be imprisoned, and he shall surely be of the utterly debased.* (12:32).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَدَخَلَ مَعَهُ السِّجْنَ فَتَيانِ** — *Two servants entered the prison with him.* (12:36) until the end of the verse. The term "servant" (الفتى) means a slave (العبد), and the context of the verses indicates that they were two slaves belonging to the King, as mentioned in the narrations that will follow, Allah willing.

His saying: **قالَ أَحَدُهُما إِنِّي أَرانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً** — *One of them said, ‘I dreamt that I was pressing wine.’* (12:36). The phrase "one of them said" is separated to indicate the interval between the narration of the dream and the entry into prison, as suggested by the context of his saying "I see myself" (أَرانِي) and his addressing him as a "companion of the prison."

His saying "I see myself" (أَرانِي) is to narrate a past state, as has been said. His saying "pressing wine" (أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً) means pressing grapes as they are pressed to be made into wine; thus, grapes are called wine in consideration of what they will become.

The meaning is: one of them woke up and said to Yusuf (peace be upon him), "I saw in a dream that I was pressing grapes for wine."

His saying: **وَقالَ الْآخَرُ إِنِّي أَرانِي أَحْمِلُ فَوْقَ رَأْسِي خُبْزاً تَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْهُ** — *And the other said, ‘I dreamt that I was carrying bread on my head from which the birds were eating.’* (12:36), meaning they were pecking at it. This is another dream mentioned by his companion. His saying: **نَبِّئْنا بِتَأْوِيلِهِ إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — *Tell us its interpretation, for we see you to be a virtuous man.* (12:36), meaning they both said, "Tell us its interpretation." The mention of the action was sufficed by saying "one said" and "the other said," which is among the subtleties of the Quranic style. The pronoun in His saying "its interpretation" (بِتَأْوِيلِهِ) refers to what they saw, as indicated by the context. In His saying "for we see you to be a virtuous man" (إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ), it is a justification for their asking for the interpretation. "We see you" (نَراكَ) means we believe you to be among the virtuous (المحسنين) because of the marks (سيما) of such people we observe in you. They approached him for the interpretation of their dreams because of his virtue, as common people believe that the virtuous and righteous possess pure hearts and sanctified souls; thus, they transition to the connections between matters and the flow of events in a manner that is better and closer to right-guidance (الرشد) than the transition of others.

The meaning is: one of them said to Yusuf, "I saw in a dream such-and-such," and the other said, "I saw such-and-such," and they said to him, "Inform us of the interpretation of what each of us saw, for we believe you are among the virtuous, and such hidden matters are not concealed from them due to the purity of their souls and the clarity of their hearts."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ لا يَأْتِيكُما طَعامٌ تُرْزَقانِهِ إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُما بِتَأْوِيلِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَأْتِيَكُما** — *He said, ‘No food which you are provided shall come to you but that I will have informed you of its interpretation before it comes to you.’* (12:37). When the two companions of the prison approached Yusuf (peace be upon him) asking about the interpretation of the dreams they had seen—out of a good opinion of him due to the marks of the virtuous they observed in him—he (peace be upon him) seized the opportunity to disseminate the secrets of monotheism (التوحيد) and the call to his Lord, the Glorified, Who had taught him that. He informed them that he was knowledgeable of that through teaching from his Lord, an expert in the interpretation of events (تأويل الأحاديث), and he used this as a means to reveal the secret of monotheism and negate partners, then he interpreted their dreams.

So he said first: **لا يَأْتِيكُما طَعامٌ تُرْزَقانِهِ** — *No food which you are provided shall come to you* — while you are in prison — **إِلَّا نَبَّأْتُكُما بِتَأْوِيلِهِ** — *but that I will have informed you of its interpretation* — meaning the interpretation of that food, its reality, and what its matter will lead to — for I am an expert in that. Let this be a sign (آية) of my truthfulness in what I call you to regarding the religion of monotheism.

This is on the assumption that the pronoun in His saying "its interpretation" (بِتَأْوِيلِهِ) refers to the food. In this case, it would be a manifestation by him (peace be upon him) of a sign of his prophethood, similar to the saying of the Messiah (peace be upon him) to the Children of Israel: **وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَما تَدَّخِرُونَ فِي بُيُوتِكُمْ إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ** — *and I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up in your houses. There is indeed a sign in that for you, should you be faithful.* (3:49). This meaning is supported by some narrations transmitted through the channels of the People of the House (peace be upon them), as will follow in the discussion of narrations, Allah willing.

As for the assumption that the pronoun in "its interpretation" (بِتَأْوِيلِهِ) refers to the dream they saw, then His saying "No food shall come to you..." etc., is a promise from him to them of the interpretation of their dreams and a promise of its swiftness. However, this meaning is not without some distance considering the context.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكما مِمَّا عَلَّمَنِي رَبِّي إِنِّي تَرَكْتُ مِلَّةَ قَوْمٍ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ وَاتَّبَعْتُ مِلَّةَ آبائِي إِبْراهِيمَ وَإِسْحاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ** — *‘That is of what my Lord has taught me. Indeed I have abandoned the creed of a people who do not believe in Allah and who are disbelievers in the Hereafter. I have followed the creed of my fathers, Abraham, Isaac and Jacob.’* (12:37-38). He (peace be upon him) explained that the knowledge and prediction of the interpretation of events is not at all from ordinary, acquired knowledge; rather, it is from what his Lord taught him. Then he justified this by his abandonment of the creed of the polytheists (المشركين) and his following the creed of his fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub—meaning his rejection of the religion of polytheism (الشرك) and his adoption of the religion of monotheism.

The polytheists among the idolaters believe in Allah, the Glorified, and affirm the Day of Recompense through the doctrine of transmigration (التناسخ), as mentioned in the previous volume of this book. However, the religion of monotheism judges that he for whom partners are designated in influence or in deservingness of worship is not Allah. Likewise, the return of souls after death in other bodies to be blessed or punished therein is not part of the Resurrection (المعاد) at all. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) negated from them belief in Allah and the Hereafter, and emphasized their disbelief in the Hereafter by repeating the pronoun when he said: "and they, in the Hereafter, they are disbelievers" (وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ هُمْ كافِرُونَ). This is because he who does not believe in Allah is even more likely not to believe in the return of the servants to Him.

This which Allah, the Glorified, narrates of Yusuf’s (peace be upon him) saying: "And I have followed the creed of my fathers Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub" is the first time he announced in Egypt his lineage and that he was from the household of Ibrahim, Ishaq, and Ya‘qub (peace be upon them).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما كانَ لَنا أَنْ نُشْرِكَ بِاللهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ ذلِكَ مِنْ فَضْلِ اللهِ عَلَيْنا وَعَلَى النَّاسِ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَشْكُرُونَ** — *‘It is not for us to ascribe any partner to Allah. That is by Allah’s grace upon us and upon mankind, but most people do not give thanks.’* (12:38). Meaning, Allah, the Glorified, did not make for us, the People of the House, a way to associate anything with Him and He prevented us from that. That prevention is from the grace and blessing of Allah upon us, the People of the House, and upon mankind, but most people do not give thanks for His grace, the Exalted, but rather disbelieve in it.

As for His making them such that they have no way to associate partners with Him, it is not a making of compulsion (إجبار) or coercion (إلجاء), but a making of support (تأييد) and guidance (تسديد), as He blessed them with prophethood (النبوة) and messengership (الرسالة)—and Allah knows best where to place His messengership. Thus, they sought protection in Allah from polytheism and adhered to monotheism.

As for that being from the grace of Allah upon them and upon mankind, it is because they were supported by the Truth, which is the best of grace, and it is within the capacity of people to return to them, thereby succeeding by following them and being guided by their guidance.

As for most people not giving thanks, it is because they disbelieve in this blessing—namely prophethood and messengership—so they pay no heed to it and do not follow its people; or because they disbelieve in the blessing of monotheism and take for Allah, the Glorified, partners from among the angels, jinn, and humans, worshipping them instead of Allah.

This is what most commentators have mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse.

However, one point remains: monotheism and the negation of partners are not matters that depend solely on the exposition of prophethood, for they are things that the intellect (العقل) independently establishes and the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — dictates. Thus, there is no meaning in counting it as a grace upon people from the perspective of following [the prophets] alone; rather, they and the prophets are on the same level and a common path regarding the matter of monotheism. If they disbelieve in monotheism, they only disbelieve because of their failure to respond to the call of the primordial nature, not because of a failure to follow the prophets.

But it must be known that just as it is necessary in the providence (عناية) of Allah, the Glorified, to equip the human species—in addition to the inspiration through the intellect of good and evil, and godwariness (التقوى) and profligacy—with that by which it perceives the rulings of its religion and the laws of its Sharia, which is the path of prophethood and revelation (الوحي), as has been repeatedly clarified in our previous discussions; likewise, it is necessary in His providence to equip individuals from among them with pure souls and sound hearts, steadfast upon their original primordial nature, adhering to His monotheism, and refraining from associating partners with Him. Through them, the origin of monotheism is preserved age after age, and the spirit of felicity (السعادة) is revived generation after generation. The proof for this is the same as the proof for prophethood and revelation. For an ordinary individual human being is not immune to polytheism and the forgetting of monotheism, and what is possible for one is possible for all. If everyone were to be clothed in polytheism, it would lead to the corruption of the species in its ultimate goal and the nullification of the divine purpose in its creation.

Therefore, it is necessary that there be men in the species clothed in the sincerity of monotheism, who uphold its cause, defend it, and awaken people from the slumber of heedlessness and ignorance by presenting its proofs and disseminating its signs and evidences. Between them and the people, there is a bond of teaching and learning, rather than mere driving and following.

These souls, if they exist, are the souls of the prophets and the Imams (peace be upon them). In their creation and their being sent is a grace from Allah, the Glorified, upon them by teaching them His monotheism, and upon mankind by appointing those who remind them of the Truth which their primordial nature dictates and who defend the Truth against their heedlessness and misguidance. For people's preoccupation with material works and their engagement with sensory matters attract them toward worldly pleasures and incite them to cling to the earth, thus distancing them from spiritual realities and making them forget the divine gnosis (المعارف الإلهية) within their primordial nature. Were it not for men who are godly and divinely enamored, whom He has purified with the pure quality of the remembrance of the Abode in every period of time, the earth would be enveloped in blindness, the link between the earth and the heavens would be severed, the purpose of creation would be nullified, and the earth would sink with its inhabitants.

From here it becomes clear that the truth is to interpret the verse according to this reality. Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: He did not make for us, by support from Allah, a way to associate anything with Allah. That—meaning our being in security from polytheism—is from the grace of Allah upon us because it is the guidance which is the felicity of man and his great triumph. And upon mankind because in that is their reminder if they forget, their awakening if they are heedless, their teaching if they are ignorant, and their straightening if they are crooked. But most people do not thank Allah, but rather disbelieve in this grace, so they pay no heed to it and do not accept it, but rather turn away from it. This is the point.

Some have mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse that what is referred to by His saying "That is by Allah’s grace upon us..." etc., is the knowledge of the interpretation of events. This, as you see, is far from the context of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا صاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَأَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ خَيْرٌ أَمِ اللهُ الْواحِدُ الْقَهَّارُ** — *‘O my two prison mates! Are different lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?’* (12:39). The word "better" (الخير), according to its weight, is an attribute from their saying: "he chose (خار), he chooses (يخار), choice (خيرة)" when he selects and chooses one of two things between which he hesitates, whether in terms of action or in terms of taking it from a certain aspect. The "better" (الخير) of the two is that which excels the other in the quality of being desired, thus making it necessary to take it. So the better of two actions is the one desired from them which must be performed, and the better of two things is the one desired from them from the aspect of taking it—like the better of two properties from the aspect of enjoying it, the better of two houses from the aspect of dwelling in it, the better of two people from the aspect of accompanying him, the better of two opinions from the aspect of adopting it, and the better of two gods from the aspect of worshipping him. Hence, the grammarians mentioned that "better" (الخير) is originally "more better" (أخير), a superlative (أفعل تفضيل), but in reality, it is an intensive adjective (صفة مشبهة) that conveys, according to its root, what the superlative conveys of excellence in comparison.

From what has passed, it becomes clear that his saying (peace be upon him): "Are different lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" etc., is intended to present the argument for His being designated for worship if the matter is assumed to be a hesitation between Him and the other lords who are called upon besides Allah. It is not to state that He, the Exalted, is the existing Truth to the exclusion of other lords, or that He, the Exalted, is the God to Whom all things return in their beginning and end to their exclusion, or other such things. For a thing is only called "better" (خيرا) from the aspect of its being sought and designated by taking it in some way. So his saying (peace be upon him): "Is He better or the other lords?" intends to ask about the designation of one of the two sides from the aspect of taking it, and taking the Lord is worshipping Him.

Furthermore, he (peace be upon him) called their deities "different lords" (أَرْبابٌ مُتَفَرِّقُونَ) because they used to worship the angels, who were in their view attributes of Allah, the Glorified, or determinations of His sacred essence to which the aspects of good and felicity in the world are attributed. Thus, they differentiated between the attributes by organizing them vertically and horizontally, worshipping each according to its specific function. So there was a god of knowledge, a god of power, a god of the heavens, a god of the earth, a god of beauty, a god of love, a god of security and fertility, and so on. They also worshipped the jinn, who are the principles of evil in the world such as death, annihilation, poverty, ugliness, pain, grief, and so on. They also worshipped individuals such as the perfected among the saints, and the tyrants among the sultans and kings, and others. All of them are "different" (متفرقون) in terms of their identities, their idols, and the statues taken for them, which were set up to turn toward them.

He contrasted the different lords by mentioning Allah, exalted is His Name, and describing Him as "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار) where he said: "or Allah, the One, the All-paramount." This phrase conveys, according to its meaning, the opposite of what is conveyed by his saying: "Are different lords...?" due to the necessity of contrast between the two sides of the hesitation.

"Allah" (الله) is a proper name by predominance (علم بالغلبة) intended to refer to the Sacred Divine Essence, which is a reality to which falsehood has no way, and an existence to which non-existence and annihilation cannot find a path. An existence of this nature cannot be assumed to have a limited boundary (حد محدود) or a defined duration (أمد ممدود), because everything limited is non-existent beyond its limit, and that which is defined is nullified after its duration. Thus, He, the Exalted, is an unlimited Essence and an infinite existence in every respect. If this is so, it is impossible to assume for Him an attribute external to His Essence and distinct from His Self—as is the case with His attributes—because this distinction would lead to His being, the Exalted, limited, not existing in the domain of the attribute, and needy, not finding the attribute in His Essence. It is also impossible to assume distinction and separation between His essential attributes such as Life, Knowledge, and Power, because that would lead to the existence of boundaries within the Essence, where what is inside a boundary is not found outside it; thus, the Essence and the attributes would become distinct, all would become multiple, and all would be limited. All of this is what polytheism (الوثنية) has acknowledged according to the knowledge of theirs that we possess.

So, what cannot be doubted by those who affirm the existence of the God, the Glorified, if they were to reflect, is that Allah, the Glorified, is existent in Himself, established by His Essence; no other is existent with this description. Whatever He possesses of the attributes of perfection is His very Essence, not added to it, nor are some of His attributes of perfection added to others; rather, He is Knowledge, Power, and Life by His very Essence.

Thus, He, the Exalted, is unitary (أحدي) in Essence and attributes; meaning He is One in His existence by His Essence—nothing is opposite Him except that it exists through Him, not independently in existence—and One in His attributes, meaning there is no real attribute for Him except that it is the very Essence. He is the One Who subdues (يقهر) everything; nothing subdues Him.

The reference to all of this is what prompted him (peace be upon him) to describe Allah, the Glorified, as "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار) where he said: "or Allah, the One, the All-paramount." Meaning that He, the Exalted, is One, but not a numerical one (واحد عددي) which, if another were added to it, would become two; rather, He is One such that it is impossible to assume an essence opposite Him unless it exists through Him, not by itself, nor to assume an attribute opposite Him unless it is His very Essence, otherwise it would be nullified. All of this is because He is an existence not limited by a boundary nor ending at a conclusion.

The argument against the opponent has been completed by him (peace be upon him) in this question by describing the lords as being "different" and Him, the Exalted, as "the One, the All-paramount." For His exalted Essence being One and All-paramount nullifies differentiation—any assumed differentiation—between the Essence and the attributes. Thus, the Essence is the very attributes, and the attributes are each the very other. So whoever worships the Essence has worshipped the Essence and the attributes, and whoever worships His Knowledge has worshipped His Essence. If he worships His Knowledge and does not worship His Essence, he has worshipped neither His Knowledge nor His Essence; and so on.

Therefore, if a hesitation in worship is assumed between different lords and Allah, the One, the All-paramount, the Exalted and Sanctified, His worship is designated to their exclusion, since it is impossible to assume different lords or differentiation in worship.

Yes, there remains one thing, which is what the general polytheists rely upon: that Allah, the Glorified, is too majestic and sublime in Essence for our intellects to encompass Him or our understandings to reach Him. Thus, we cannot turn to Him with our worship, nor is it within our capacity to draw near to Him through our servitude and submission. What is within our capacity is to draw near through worship to some of His noble creatures who are influential in the management of the world's order, so that they may bring us closer to Him and intercede for us with Him. So he (peace be upon him) pointed in the second part of his speech—namely his saying: "You do not worship besides Him except names..." etc.—to its refutation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما تَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِهِ إِلَّا أَسْماءً سَمَّيْتُمُوها أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ** — *‘You do not worship besides Him but names that you and your fathers have coined, for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah; He has commanded you to worship none except Him.’* (12:40) etc. He (peace be upon him) began by addressing his two companions in prison first, then generalized the address to everyone because the ruling is common between them and other idolaters.

The negation of worship except for "names" (الأسماء) is a metaphor for the fact that there are no named entities (مسميات) behind these names. Thus, the worship falls upon the names, such as the term "god of the heaven," "god of the earth," "god of the sea," "god of the land," "the father," "the mother," "the son of god," and the like.

He emphasized that these names have no named entities behind them by his saying: "you and your fathers" (أَنْتُمْ وَآباؤُكُمْ), for it is in the sense of restriction (حصر)—meaning no one besides you and your fathers coined these names. Then he emphasized it a second time by his saying: "for which Allah has not sent down any authority" (ما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ بِها مِنْ سُلْطانٍ). "Authority" (السلطان) is the proof (البرهان) because of its authority over the intellects. Meaning, Allah has not sent down any proof for these names or for this naming that indicates they have named entities behind them; in which case, divinity (الألوهية)—meaning the quality of being worshipped—would be established for them, and your worship of them would be correct.

It is possible that the pronoun in "for which" (بِها) refers to the worship; meaning Allah has not sent down any proof for their worship by establishing for them intercession and independence in influence such that their worship and turning toward them would be correct. For the matter belongs to Allah in any case. To this he pointed by his saying afterward: "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah" (إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ).

This—namely his saying: "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah"—is something about which there is no doubt at all. For sovereignty (الحكم) in any matter is only valid from one who possesses total disposal (التصرف), and there is no possessor of disposal and management in the affairs of the world and the cultivation of the servants in reality except Allah, the Glorified. Thus, there is no sovereignty in the true sense of the meaning except for Him.

This—namely his saying: "Sovereignty belongs only to Allah"—is useful for what precedes it and what follows it, being suitable as a justification for both. As for its utility in his saying before: "for which Allah has not sent down any authority," it has just become clear. As for its utility in his saying after: "He has commanded you to worship none except Him," it is because it encompasses the aspect of affirming the sovereignty, just as his saying before: "for which Allah has not sent down any authority" encompasses the aspect of negation. His sovereignty, the Exalted, is effective in both aspects together. It is as if when it was said: "Allah has not sent down any authority for them," it was asked: "Then what has He decreed regarding the matter of worship?" So it was said: "He has commanded you to worship none except Him." Therefore, the verb was used.

The meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is: You do not worship besides Allah except names devoid of named entities, which none but you and your fathers have coined, without Allah, the Glorified, sending down from Him any proof indicating that they have intercession with Allah or any independence in influence, such that it would be correct for you to claim their worship to attain their intercession, or out of desire for their good or fear of their evil.

As for his saying: **ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — *‘That is the upright religion, but most people do not know.’* (12:40). He refers by this to what he mentioned of the monotheism of Allah and the negation of partners for Him. "Upright" (القيم) is that which stands over the matter, strong in its management, or that which stands on its own leg, not wavering or faltering. The meaning is that the religion of monotheism alone is the one strong enough to manage society and lead it to the abode of felicity; it is the firm religion, not wavering, in which there is right-guidance without error and truthfulness without falsehood. But most people, because of their familiarity with the sensory and the perceived and their immersion in the ornaments of the vanishing world, have been deprived of the soundness of the heart and the rectitude of the intellect; they do not know that. They only know the outward appearance of the life of this world, while they are heedless of the Hereafter.

As for monotheism being a religion in which there is right-guidance and correspondence with reality, the proof he (peace be upon him) established for it is sufficient in its exposition. As for it being the one strong enough to manage human society, it is because this species only finds felicity in the course of its life if it builds the norms of its life and the laws of its livelihood upon a true foundation corresponding to reality, and proceeds upon them; not if it builds them upon a false, superstitious foundation that does not rely on a firm origin.

It has become clear from all that has preceded that both verses—namely from his saying: "O my two prison mates!" to his saying: "to worship none except Him"—constitute a single proof for the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة). Its summary is that if the worship of the worshipped is for its divinity in itself and the necessity of its existence by its essence, then Allah, the Glorified, in His existence is One and All-paramount; no second can be conceived for Him, nor is there any other influencer alongside His influence. Thus, there is no meaning to the multiplicity of gods. And if it is because gods other than Allah are partners to Him and intercessors with Him, then there is no evidence for the establishment of intercession for them from Allah, the Glorified; rather, the evidence is to the contrary, for Allah has decreed through the intellect and by the tongues of His prophets that none should be worshipped but He.

By this, the invalidity of what al-Baydawi mentioned in his commentary—following *al-Kashshaf*—becomes clear: that the two verses encompass two proofs for monotheism. Thus, what is in the first, which is his saying: "Are different lords better, or Allah, the One, the All-paramount?" is a rhetorical proof (دليل خطابي), and what is in the second, which is his saying: "You do not worship besides Him but names..." etc., is a complete demonstration (برهان تام).

Al-Baydawi said: "This is from the gradual progression in the call and the imposition of the argument. He explained to them first the preference for monotheism over the adoption of gods by way of rhetoric, then he demonstrated that what they call gods and worship do not deserve divinity. For the deservingness of worship is either by essence or by another, and both categories are negated from them. Then he explicitly stated what is the upright truth and the straight religion, which the intellect requires nothing else and knowledge accepts nothing besides." [End of quote].

Perhaps what led him to this was the word "better" (الخير) in the first verse, so he inferred from it a rhetorical preference. He missed what is in it of the qualification "the One, the All-paramount" (الواحد القهار). You have already recognized the presentation of what the two verses encompass of the demonstration, and that what he mentioned of the meaning of the second verse is the significance of the sum of the two verses, not the second one alone.

Perhaps the significance of the two verses can be presented as two proofs for monotheism in another way, summarized as follows: Allah, the One, Who subdues by His power the different causes that act in the universe and leads them toward the harmony of their different and diverse effects with one another until a single order is organized from them, not contradictory in its parts—as is witnessed from the unity of the order and the agreement of the causes—is better than different lords from whom, due to their difference and opposition, different systems and contradictory managements would emanate, leading to the rupture of the unity of the cosmic order and the corruption of the single, general management.

Then, the gods worshipped besides Allah are names for which there is no evidence for the existence of their named entities in external reality through your naming, neither from the side of the intellect nor from the side of transmission (النقل). For the intellect does not indicate anything but monotheism, and the prophets were not commanded through revelation except that none should be worshipped but Allah alone. [End of quote].

This presentation—as you see—interprets the first verse according to the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **لَوْ كانَ فِيهِما آلِهَةٌ إِلَّا اللهُ لَفَسَدَتا** — *‘Had there been any gods in them besides Allah, they would surely have fallen apart.’* (21:22), and generalizes the second verse to the negation of the divinity of gods besides Allah by their essence and the negation of their divinity from the aspect of Allah's permission for their intercession.

Against this, it is objected: First, that it contains a restriction of the absolute nature of His saying "the All-paramount" (الْقَهَّارُ) without a restrictor. For Allah, the Glorified, just as He subdues the causes in their influence, He subdues everything in its essence, attribute, and effects. Thus, there is no second to Him in His existence, nor is there a second to Him in His independence in Himself and in His influence. It is not possible, given His absolute subduing unity, to assume anything independent of Him in its existence, nor any matter independent of Him in its command. The god that is assumed besides Him is either independent of Him in both essence and the effects of essence together, or independent of Him in the effects of essence alone; and both matters are impossible, as has been shown.

Second, that it contains a generalization of the specific nature of the second verse without a generalizer. For the verse—as you have recognized—links their being gods to the permission and sovereignty of Allah, as is apparent in His saying: "for which Allah has not sent down any authority. Sovereignty belongs only to Allah..." etc. It is clear that this divinity linked to His permission, the Exalted, and His sovereignty is a divinity of intercession, not an essential divinity—meaning a divinity by another, not what is more general than divinity by essence and by another together.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا صاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَمَّا أَحَدُكُما فَيَسْقِي رَبَّهُ خَمْراً وَأَمَّا الْآخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْ رَأْسِهِ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ** — *‘O my two prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master, and as for the other, he will be crucified and the birds will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided.’* (12:41). The meaning of the verse is clear. The context of suitability dictates that his saying: "As for one of you..." etc., is the interpretation of the dream of the one who said: "I dreamt that I was pressing wine," and his saying: "and as for the other..." etc., is the interpretation of the dream of the other.

His saying: "The matter about which you inquire has been decided" (قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ) is not without an indication that the two companions, or one of them, belied himself in his claim of the dream—perhaps the second one when he heard the interpretation of his dream as crucifixion and birds eating from his head. This is supported by what has been narrated through the channels of the Imams of the People of the House (peace be upon them): that the second of the two companions said to him, "I lied in what I narrated to you of the dream." So he (peace be upon him) said: "The matter about which you inquire has been decided," meaning that the interpretation about which you both inquired is decided, certain, and there is no escape from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ لِلَّذِي ظَنَّ أَنَّهُ ناجٍ مِنْهُمَا اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ فَأَنْساهُ الشَّيْطانُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِ فَلَبِثَ فِي السِّجْنِ بِضْعَ سِنِينَ** — *And he said to the one of the two whom he knew would be delivered, ‘Mention me to your master.’ But Satan caused him to forget mentioning it to his master. So he remained in the prison for several years.* (12:42). The pronouns in His saying "he said" (قالَ), "he knew/thought" (ظَنَ), and "he remained" (فَلَبِثَ) refer to Yusuf. That is, Yusuf said to the one whom he believed would be saved of the two: "Mention me to your master" in a way that stirs his mercy, so that perhaps he might release me from prison.

The use of the term "thought/believed" (الظن) for his conviction, despite his explicit statement to them that it was from what is decided and certain, and his explicit statement that his Lord taught him the interpretation of events, is perhaps from the use of "thought" for absolute conviction (الاعتقاد). It has parallels in the Quran, such as His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَظُنُّونَ أَنَّهُمْ مُلاقُوا رَبِّهِمْ** — *those who are certain that they will encounter their Lord* (2:46).

As for the saying of some that the use of "thought" for his conviction indicates that he only interpreted what he interpreted based on his own exertion of judgment (اجتهاد), it is invalidated by what we previously pointed out: that he explicitly stated his knowledge in his saying: "The matter about which you inquire has been decided," and Allah, the Glorified, supported that by His saying: "and that We might teach him the interpretation of events" (وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأَحادِيثِ). This contradicts conjectural exertion of judgment.

It has been considered that the pronoun in "he knew/thought" (ظَنَ) refers to the relative pronoun; meaning Yusuf said to his companion who thought—that companion—that he would be saved of the two. This meaning is not objectionable if the context supports it.

His saying: **فأَنْساهُ الشَّيْطانُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِ** — *But Satan caused him to forget mentioning it to his master.* Both pronouns refer to "the one [who was delivered]"; meaning Satan caused his companion who was saved to forget to mention him to his master or in the presence of his master. Thus, Yusuf remained in prison for several years (بضع سنين), and "several" (البضع) is what is less than ten. The addition of "mentioning" (الذكر) to "his master" (ربه) is of the type of adding the verbal noun to its object to which it is transitive by a particle, or to the circumstance through some kind of connection.

As for referring both pronouns to Yusuf, such that it would mean Satan caused Yusuf to forget the remembrance of Allah, the Glorified, so he clung to the coattails of another for his deliverance from prison and was punished for that, remaining in prison for several years—as mentioned by some and perhaps attributed to narration—this is among the things that contradict the explicit text of the Book. For Allah, the Glorified, explicitly stated that he (peace be upon him) was among the "sincere ones" (المخلصين), and He explicitly stated that Satan has no way to the sincere ones, in addition to the praise Allah bestowed upon him in this Surah.

Sincerity (الإخلاص) toward Allah does not necessitate abandoning the use of means (التوسل بالأسباب), for that would be from the greatest ignorance as it is a desire for what cannot be desired; rather, it only necessitates abandoning trust in them and reliance upon them. There is nothing in his saying: "Mention me to your master" that suggests that at all.

Furthermore, His saying, the Exalted, two verses later: **وَقالَ الَّذِي نَجا مِنْهُما وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ** — *The one of the two who had been delivered and who remembered after a long time, said...* (12:45) etc., is a suitable context indicating that the one who forgot was the cupbearer, not Yusuf.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding His saying: **ثُمَّ بَدا لَهُمْ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما رَأَوُا الْآياتِ لَيَسْجُنُنَّهُ حَتَّى حِينٍ** — *Then it occurred to them, after they had seen the signs, to imprison him for a while.* (12:35). He said: "The signs were the testimony of the infant, the shirt torn from behind, and their racing to the door until he heard her pulling him at the door. When he disobeyed her, she did not cease inciting her husband until he imprisoned him."

"And two servants entered the prison with him"—he says: two slaves of the King—one was a baker and the other was the cupbearer. The one who lied and did not see the dream was the baker.

The narration was mentioned by ‘Ali ibn Ibrahim al-Qummi, who said: "The King entrusted two men to Yusuf to guard him. When they entered the prison, they said to him: 'What is your craft?' He said: 'I interpret dreams.' One of the guards saw in his dream—as he said—that he was pressing wine. Yusuf said: 'You will go out and become the King's cupbearer, and your status with him will rise.' The other said: 'I dreamt that I was carrying bread on my head from which the birds were eating,' and he had not seen that. Yusuf said to him: 'The King will kill you and crucify you, and the birds will eat from your head.' The man laughed and said: 'I did not see that.' So Yusuf said as Allah narrated: **يا صاحِبَيِ السِّجْنِ أَمَّا أَحَدُكُما فَيَسْقِي رَبَّهُ خَمْراً وَأَمَّا الْآخَرُ فَيُصْلَبُ فَتَأْكُلُ الطَّيْرُ مِنْ رَأْسِهِ قُضِيَ الْأَمْرُ الَّذِي فِيهِ تَسْتَفْتِيانِ** — *‘O my two prison mates! As for one of you, he will serve wine to his master, and as for the other, he will be crucified and the birds will eat from his head. The matter about which you inquire has been decided.’* (12:41)."

Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) said regarding His saying: **إِنَّا نَراكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — *for we see you to be a virtuous man.* (12:36). He said: "He used to attend to the sick, seek for the needy, and provide ease for the imprisoned. When the one who saw in his dream that he was pressing wine intended to leave the prison, Yusuf said to him: **اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ** — *‘Mention me to your master.’* (12:42). So it was as Allah said: **فَأَنْساهُ الشَّيْطانُ ذِكْرَ رَبِّهِ** — *But Satan caused him to forget mentioning it to his master.* (12:42)."

**I say:** In the narration, there is a verbal disorder. Its outward meaning is that his two companions in prison were not prisoners, but were guards over him from the King. This does not harmonize with the outward meaning of His saying, the Exalted: "And he said to the one of the two whom he knew would be delivered" and His saying: "The one of the two who had been delivered said."

In the *Tafsir* of al-‘Ayyashi, from Sama‘ah: "Regarding the saying of Allah: **اذْكُرْنِي عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ** — *‘Mention me to your master.’* (12:42). He said: 'He is the Governor (العزيز).'"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi al-Dunya in the *Book of Punishments*, and Ibn Jarir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: 'Had Yusuf not said the word that he said, he would not have remained in prison as long as he remained—when he sought relief from other than Allah, the Exalted.'"

**I say:** It was also narrated by Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Hurayrah from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and its wording is: "May Allah have mercy on Yusuf; had he not said: 'Mention me to your master,' he would not have remained in prison as long as he remained." Similar to it was narrated from ‘Ikrimah, al-Hasan, and others.

Al-‘Ayyashi narrated what is in its meaning in his *Tafsir* from Tirbal, from Ibn Abi Ya‘qub, and from Ya‘qub ibn Shu‘ayb, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him). The wording of the latter is: "Allah said to Yusuf: 'Am I not the One Who made you beloved to your father and preferred you over people with beauty? Am I not the One Who led the caravan to you, so I rescued you and brought you out of the pit? Am I not the One Who diverted from you the plot of the women? So what carried you to raise a subject or call upon a creature who is below Me? So remain, for what you said, for several years.'" It has already been mentioned that these and their like are narrations that contradict the explicit text of the Book.

Similar to them is what is in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Ibn Mardawayh from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: "Yusuf (peace be upon him) stumbled three times: his saying: 'Mention me to your master'; his saying to his brothers: 'Indeed you are thieves'; and his saying: 'That he may know that I did not betray him in his absence.' So Jibra’il said to him: 'Not even when you intended?' So he said: 'And I do not absolve my own soul.'" In the narration is the attribution of slander and explicit lying to the Truthful One (الصديق) (peace be upon him).

In some of these narrations, his three stumbles were: his intention for her, his saying: "Mention me to your master," and his saying: "Indeed you are thieves." Allah, the Glorified, absolves him of these fabrications by the explicit text of His Book.
